coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of porous biochar supported Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst for efficient degradation of ciprofloxacin in seawater under visible light irradiation: Mechanistic investigation and intermediates analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Yuzhu
Wang, Jiahui
Ma, Jinku
Kong, Feng
Xue, Jinjuan
Ye, Zhaolian
Ma, Shuaishuai
Zhao, Songjian
coredata.dc:description: Efficient separation of photocarriers is a critical approach for advancing high-performance photocatalyst systems to effectively remove environmental contaminants. In this investigation, a hydrothermal technique was employed to synthesize composites of Bi2MoO6 and porous biochar derived from banana peels (BPB). The samples underwent various characterization methods to assess their composition, morphology, and photoelectrochemical properties. The BPB/Bi2MoO6 composite exhibited a synergistic adsorption and photocatalytic impact in eliminating the antibiotic ciprofloxacin (CIP) from pure water and seawater. The optimized BPB/Bi2MoO6 sample displayed a significant degradation efficiency of 98.7 % for CIP after 90 min of visible-light exposure in pure water. In artificial seawater, the degradation efficiency for CIP remained high at 93.6 %, which was 12.2 times greater than that of pure Bi2MoO6. The study also explored the impact of various factors such as catalyst dosage, solution pH, and pollutant concentration on the photocatalytic performance of BPB/Bi2MoO6. Moreover, the catalyst exhibited enhanced efficiency in the adsorption-photocatalytic degradation of CIP within a continuous flow reaction system. The enhanced photocatalytic performance was attributed to the composite's exceptional adsorption capacity, enhanced response to visible light, and efficient separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. Furthermore, the BPB/Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst displayed good stability and recyclability over five consecutive photodegradation cycles. Radical trapping and electron spin resonance (ESR) experiments verified that the degradation of CIP was mainly driven by •OH and h+ radicals. A thorough investigation employing density functional theory (DFT), radical trapping and ESR tests, and liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (LC-MS) was conducted to elucidate the degradation mechanism and pathways. Furthermore, toxicity assessment software was utilized to illustrate the progressive reduction in toxicity of the intermediate products. In conclusion, this study provides valuable insights into the role of biochar in enhancing the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants and offers guidance for the future development of environmentally friendly photocatalysts.

Scheme illustration of continuous flow system.
XRD patterns of Bi2MoO6 and BPB/Bi2MoO6 with different composite ratios (a) and Raman spectra of Bi2MoO6, BPB and BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) (b).
SEM images of (a) BPB, (b) Bi2MoO6, (c) BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4); (d, e) elemental mapping images and (f) EDS analysis of BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4).

(a) Effects of catalyst dosage, (b) initial CIP concentration, (c) initial pH and (d) main components in artificial seawater on the degradation efficiency of CIP over BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4). Experimental conditions: except the exact analyzing factor, other factors were fixed with [catalyst] = 0.2 g·L-1, [CIP] = 10 mg·L-1, temperature = 25 °C, and initial pH without adjustment.

(a) Photoluminescence spectra, (b) photocurrent responses and (c) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (ESI) analysis of Bi2MoO6 and BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4); (d) Trapping experiment of different free radical over BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4); ESR spin-trapping spectra of Bi2MoO6 and BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) for DMPO-•OH (e) and DMPO-•O2 – (f) under dark and visible light irradiation for 10 min.

Mechanisms of CIP removal by BPB/Bi2MoO6 under light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of porous biochar supported Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst for efficient degradation of ciprofloxacin in seawater under visible light irradiation: Mechanistic investigation and intermediates analysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficient separation of photocarriers is a critical approach for advancing high-performance photocatalyst systems to effectively remove environmental contaminants. In this investigation, a hydrothermal technique was employed to synthesize composites of Bi2MoO6 and porous biochar derived from banana peels (BPB). The samples underwent various characterization methods to assess their composition, morphology, and photoelectrochemical properties. The BPB/Bi2MoO6 composite exhibited a synergistic adsorption and photocatalytic impact in eliminating the antibiotic ciprofloxacin (CIP) from pure water and seawater. The optimized BPB/Bi2MoO6 sample displayed a significant degradation efficiency of 98.7 % for CIP after 90 min of visible-light exposure in pure water. In artificial seawater, the degradation efficiency for CIP remained high at 93.6 %, which was 12.2 times greater than that of pure Bi2MoO6. The study also explored the impact of various factors such as catalyst dosage, solution pH, and pollutant concentration on the photocatalytic performance of BPB/Bi2MoO6. Moreover, the catalyst exhibited enhanced efficiency in the adsorption-photocatalytic degradation of CIP within a continuous flow reaction system. The enhanced photocatalytic performance was attributed to the composite's exceptional adsorption capacity, enhanced response to visible light, and efficient separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. Furthermore, the BPB/Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst displayed good stability and recyclability over five consecutive photodegradation cycles. Radical trapping and electron spin resonance (ESR) experiments verified that the degradation of CIP was mainly driven by •OH and h+ radicals. A thorough investigation employing density functional theory (DFT), radical trapping and ESR tests, and liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (LC-MS) was conducted to elucidate the degradation mechanism and pathways. Furthermore, toxicity assessment software was utilized to illustrate the progressive reduction in toxicity of the intermediate products. In conclusion, this study provides valuable insights into the role of biochar in enhancing the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants and offers guidance for the future development of environmentally friendly photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: The mariculture industry has seen substantial growth in response to the increasing demand for seafood, leading to the establishment of numerous mariculture sites for cultivating various seafood species [1,2]. However, it is crucial to acknowledge the potential risk of infectious disease outbreaks in these cultured waters due to the presence of bacteria in seawater. As a result, antibiotics like penicillin, oxytetracycline, and tetracycline are commonly used in mariculture to prevent and control infectious diseases [3–5]. Nevertheless, the significant toxicity of these antibiotics poses challenges in their degradation within organisms, causing non-standard decomposition and water pollution [6,7]. Prolonged exposure to antibiotic-laden wastewater also raises concerns about genetic contamination and ecological disruption. To combat these issues, researchers have developed technologies such as biological oxidation, adsorption, catalytic oxidation, and membrane separation to efficiently treat seawater contaminated with antibiotics [8–10]. Despite their effectiveness, these methods often face limitations in degradation efficiency and high application costs, hindering further progress. In contrast, photocatalysis offers a sophisticated oxidation technology that utilizes photoactive semiconductor materials to generate active substances upon absorbing solar energy [11,12]. The active components react with contaminants on the surface, facilitating effective processing. This method holds great promise for combating water pollution in mariculture locations, as well as utilizing sustainable energy sources for eco-friendly practices. As a result, there is a critical necessity to create low-cost and effective semiconductor photocatalysts.

To date, a variety of Bi-based semiconductors, such as Bi2MoO6, Bi2WO6, BiVO4, and BiOBr, have been utilized in organic remediation due to their advantageous band energy, chemical stability, controlled crystal phase, and response to visible light [13–16]. Bi2MoO6, a notable Aurivillius-structured Bi-based semiconductor, has shown great promise in environmental remediation due to its chemical and thermal stability, layered structure, and wide light absorption range [17,18]. The hydrothermal method is commonly used for synthesizing Bi2MoO6 [14,19], allowing precise control over the morphology, crystal structure, and catalytic properties of nanomaterials in a sealed environment by adjusting temperature and pressure. However, challenges like inefficient separation of charge carriers, low specific surface areas, and limited adsorption capabilities hinder the practical application of Bi2MoO6 in photocatalysis [19,20]. Numerous modification strategies have been devised to alter the electronic structure of Bi2MoO6 for improved photocatalytic performance, such as doping, metal deposition, surface modification, and the creation of heterogeneous or composite structures [21–24]. Among these, the formation of composites by combining with other materials is considered a viable and promising approach. Nevertheless, challenges persist in terms of complex preparation procedures and high expenses, highlighting the need for the investigation of simpler and more cost-effective modification methods. Biochar, produced through pyrolysis of sustainable raw materials, possesses high carbon content, a large specific surface area, diverse surface functional groups, cost-effectiveness and well-developed porous structures [25–27]. This allows biochar to effectively adsorb organic pollutants and potentially be used in various applications, such as electromagnetic composites [28,29]. Additionally, biochars exhibit notable electrical conductivity, reducing electron and hole recombination during the photocatalytic process, thereby enhancing photocatalytic efficiency [30]. This suggests that biochar could serve as a promising alternative to commercially available activated carbon, graphene oxide (GO), carbon dots, graphite carbonitride (g-C3N4), and other carbon-based support materials. For instance, Pinna et al. utilized biochar produced from microalgae pyrolysis as a co-catalyst with TiO2 to enhance the photodegradation of methylene blue, resulting in a threefold increase in removal compared to pristine TiO2, attributed to synergistic photocatalysis and adsorption effects [31]. Gómez-Avilés et al. found that the addition of carbon into TiO2 composites using lignin as a carbon source through a hydrothermal method resulted in enhanced photocatalytic activity due to the structural defects induced by the carbon incorporation [32]. Li et al. utilized a sol–gel pyrolysis approach to produce LaFeO3/lignin-biochar composites, which demonstrated superior adsorption and degradation properties attributed to the synergistic effects of LaFeO3 nanoparticles and lignin-biochar [33].

This study focuses on utilizing banana peels (BP) as a substrate for creating porous biochar (BPB) and forming BPB/Bi2MoO6 composites through a hydrothermal approach. These hybrids are then used for the degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) in ocean water under visible light exposure. The research objectives include: (1) preparing and analyzing the crystalline structure, surface morphology, and elemental composition of BPB/Bi2MoO6 composites; (2) assessing the catalytic efficiency of these hybrids in the photodegradation of CIP in seawater; (3) investigating the activation and synergy mechanisms between Bi2MoO6 and BPB through radical quenching trials, electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis, density functional theory (DFT) calculations, and electrochemical assessments; and (4) analyzing the byproducts generated after oxidation using liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (LC-MS) and proposing potential degradation pathways.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BPB and BPB/Bi2MoO6 composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 depicts a streamlined synthetic pathway. Initially, the banana peel (BP) was fragmented and desiccated at 60 °C for 24 h before being pulverized. Subsequently, a blend of BP (5 g), KOH (2 g), and ethyl alcohol (5 mL) was placed in a 300 mL agate vessel, along with 200 g of balls. The ball milling regimen was programmed at 500 rpm for 2 h, with the rotational direction changing every 30 min. Following completion, the milled powder was transferred to a crucible and subjected to calcination under N2 flow at 650 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 5 oC·min−1. The resulting material, porous biochar derived from banana peel (BPB), was rinsed with 1 mol·L-1 HCl until the filtrate reached a pH of approximately 7.0, and then dried. Subsequently, BPB/Bi2MoO6 composites were synthesized via a template-free hydrothermal method. Initially, 0.9705 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water (solution A) with stirring for 20 min. Subsequently, 0.2420 g of Na2MoO4·2H2O and a specified amount of BPB were added to 30 mL of deionized water (solution B) and sonicated for 1 h. The resulting suspension, achieved by amalgamating solutions A and B, was then transferred to a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor and heated at 180 °C for 16 h. The resultant products were recovered via vacuum filtration, washed with deionized water and ethanol, and dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h. Moreover, BPB/Bi2MoO6 composites with varying ratios of Bi2MoO6 to BPB (2:1, 4:1, 6:1, 8:1, and 10:1) were synthesized.

The SmartLab diffractometer (Rigaku) was utilized to identify the crystalline phases, employing Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). Scanning electron microscope (SEM, Sigma 500) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F) were performed to investigate the morphologies and microstructures of crystal. X-ray photoelectron spectrometers (ESCALAB 250xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) were utilized to obtain the elements contents and their chemical states. The graphitization degree of the samples was analyzed using Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw inVia Reflex, UK). The BET surface area was calculated based on the adsorption isotherm (ASAP2020HD88). Optical properties were analyzed with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu). Photoluminescence spectra were recorded with a F-7000 spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) using an excitation wavelength of 372 nm. Electron spin resonance (ESR) studies were conducted with a MS-5000X ESR spectrometer Miniscope (Magnettech, Germany) to detect reactive species generated in the photocatalytic system under visible light irradiation, using 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as the radicals spin-trapped reagent. The photoelectrochemical performances were investigated using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation with a 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution as the electrolyte. The electrical resistivities of the BPB was tested using a resistivity tester (ST2253y). Agilent 6410 Liquid Chromatography Mass Spectrometer (LC-MS, USA) was employed to identify the degradation intermediates of ciprofloxacin (CIP) in aqueous solution by the as-prepared sample under visible light irradiation.

The Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) was utilized for density functional theory (DFT) calculations within the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) utilizing the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) formulation [34–36]. Projected augmented wave (PAW) potentials were chosen to depict the ionic cores and account for valence electrons. A plane wave basis set with a kinetic energy cutoff of 450 eV was utilized [37,38]. Gaussian smearing method with a 0.05 eV width was employed for partial occupancies of the Kohn-Sham orbitals. Brillouin zone integration for geometry and lattice size optimization was conducted with a 0.04 Å−1 k-mesh Gamma centered sampling [39]. A convergence energy threshold of 10-5 eV was applied in the self-consistent calculations. Equilibrium geometries and lattice constants were optimized with a maximum stress of 0.02 eV Å−1 per atom. A 17 Å vacuum layer was added to the surface to prevent artificial interactions between periodic images. DFT + D3 method with empirical correction in Grimme's scheme was used to describe weak interactions [40,41]. Spin polarization method was employed for the magnetic system. To address the strong correlation effects of transition metals, a spin-dependent GGA plus Hubbard correction U method was utilized, with an effective Ueff parameter of 4.2 eV for Mo atom.

In order to assess the effectiveness of the prepared photocatalysts in degrading CIP, experiments were conducted in a photoreactor with a quartz jacket and water circulation at ambient temperature. Specifically, 20 mg of the photocatalyst produced was dispersed in 100 mL of simulated seawater (containing 25 g·L-1 NaCl, 11 g·L-1 MgCl2, 4 g·L-1 Na2SO4, and 1.6 g·L-1 CaCl2 in deionized water) with an initial CIP concentration of 10 mg·L-1. Following a 60-minute dark shaking period to establish adsorption–desorption equilibrium, the resulting mixture was exposed to visible light from a Xenon lamp (300 W) with an ultraviolet filter (≥400 nm) positioned approximately 10 cm away. Samples were collected every 10 min, centrifuged to separate the photocatalyst, and the absorbance of the reaction solution was measured using UV–vis spectroscopy at 353 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalysis in a continuous flow system
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 2 , a 300 W Xenon lamp equipped with a 400 nm cut-off filter was positioned 15 cm above the reactor to serve as the primary light source. The 0.125 g catalyst was dispersed ultrasonically in 5 mL of ultrapure water, and then slowly dropped onto the frosted surface of a glass plate (3.8 cm × 1.8 cm). The suspension was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h before being placed into a customized reactor measuring 4 cm in length, 2 cm in width, and 1.5 cm in height. The antibiotics-laden artificial seawater and the reactor were linked via a peristaltic pump that ran at a flow rate of 2 mL·min−1. The levels of antibiotics in the reactor were continuously monitored using a UV–vis spectrophotometer to track changes over time.

X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) was employed to investigate the crystalline structure of BPB, Bi2MoO6, and their composites. The XRD patterns are depicted in Fig. 3 a and Fig. S1. It is noteworthy that Fig. S1 shows two broad overlapping peaks at 23.3° and 43.4° for BPB, corresponding to the (002) and (101) planes of amorphous phase activated carbon [42]. For pure Bi2MoO6, the XRD pattern exhibits five distinct peaks located at around 28.3°, 32.6°, 47.1°, 55.6°, and 58.4°, representing the (131), (002), (202), (133), and (262) planes of orthorhombic Bi2MoO6 (JCPDS 84–0787). The XRD peaks for the BPB/Bi2MoO6 composites were found to be identical to those of the orthorhombic Bi2MoO6 phase, with no additional impurity peaks observed. This confirms that the incorporation of BPB does not modify the crystal structure of Bi2MoO6. Raman spectroscopy is a valuable technique for analyzing the local structure of materials due to its ability to provide insights into the bonding configurations within a material's coordination polyhedra. Fig. 3b illustrates the Raman spectra of Bi2MoO6 and BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4). In the case of Bi2MoO6, vibrations below 180 cm−1 likely correspond to the movement of molybdenum and bismuth atoms [43]. For instance, the peak at 145 cm−1 represents the lattice vibration of Bi3+ atoms along the perpendicular direction to the layers [44]. The signals within the 180–500 cm−1 range are attributed to the bending vibrations of MoO6 octahedra coupled with the stretching and bending motions of the bismuth-oxygen polyhedra. Notably, the prominent Raman peaks at around 290 cm−1 are related to the Eg bending vibrations, while those at 328 cm−1 and 356 cm−1 are indicative of the Eu symmetric bending [45]. The A1g peak at 797 cm−1 is linked to the symmetric stretching of a MoO6 octahedron, whereas the peaks at 719 cm−1 and 848 cm−1 (A2u mode) suggest the orthorhombic distortions of MoO6 octahedra within the Bi2MoO6 structure featuring layered slabs connected by distorted MoO6 octahedra [46,47]. Both the disordered (D) band at 1345 cm−1 and the graphitic (G) band at 1595 cm−1 are observed in the spectra of BPB and BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) samples. The D band typically signifies structural flaws and partially disordered regions within sp2 domains, whereas the G band corresponds to the E2g vibrational mode of graphitic sp2 carbon domains [48]. The calculated ID/IG ratios for BPB and BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) are found to be 1.09 and 1.37, respectively. The higher ID/IG value for BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) compared to BPB suggests an increase in defects upon the combination with Bi2MoO6, which could potentially enhance the conductivity and generate active sites [49].

The scanning electron microscope (SEM) micrographs in Fig. 4 depict the structures of BPB, Bi2MoO6, and the BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) composite. In Fig. 4a, the image shows that BPB has a folded flake-shaped structure with a dense microporous configuration, indicating its potential as a support for Bi2MoO6. Additionally, the surface microporous structure of BPB enhances its adsorption capabilities for organic pollutants. The SEM image in Fig. 4b illustrates that the bare Bi2MoO6 sample has a nanoplate-like morphology with a side length ranging from 50-130 nm. Fig. 4c demonstrates that a significant quantity of Bi2MoO6 nanoplates can be effectively loaded onto the surface of BPB, showcasing the composite's potential for enhancing photocatalytic performance. Further analysis using EDS and elemental mapping of C, O, Bi, and Mo (Fig. 4d–f) confirms that the BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) composite is composed of Bi, Mo, O, and C elements, with Bi and Mo evenly distributed on the surface, indicating successful incorporation of Bi2MoO6 onto the BPB. Fig. S2 delves into the detailed microstructure of BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) through TEM and HRTEM analysis. In Fig. S2a, smaller Bi2MoO6 nanoplates are observed stacked on top of the larger sheet-like BPB surface, forming an interconnected structure that facilitates charge transfer during the reaction. The HRTEM images in Fig. S2b reveal lattice fringes with spacings of 0.315 nm corresponding to the Bi2MoO6 (131) crystal plane, while the amorphous nature of BPB is evident from the absence of lattice fringes.

XPS was used to characterize the surface element composition and chemical state of BPB, Bi2MoO6, and BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4). Analysis of the XPS full spectrum for these materials (Fig. S3) revealed the presence of Bi, Mo, O, and C elements, as anticipated. When examining pure Bi2MoO6, the Bi 4f spectrum (Fig. 5 a) exhibited two peaks at 159.1 and 164.5 eV, derived from Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 in Bi (III) species [50,51]. In the BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) composite, the Bi 4f peaks were observed at a positive energy compared to Bi2MoO6, indicative of reduced electron density on the Bi atoms [52]. The Mo 3d XPS spectra (Fig. 5b) displayed peaks at 231.9 and 235.1 eV, corresponding to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 of Mo6+ [53,54]. Notably, in the BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) composite, the Mo 3d peaks were also shifted to a positive energy relative to Bi2MoO6. Analysis of the O 1s peaks (Fig. 5c) in Bi2MoO6 showed two distinct peaks for lattice oxygen (529.9 eV) and surface chemisorbed oxygen (530.9 eV). In the BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) composite, the lattice oxygen peak shifted positively compared to Bi2MoO6, indicating decreased electron density on the O atoms of Bi2MoO6 [55]. In the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 5d), the wide asymmetric peak was deconvoluted into peaks at 284.8 eV (C = C), 286.3 eV (C-O), and 288.6 eV (C = O) [56]. From the XPS results, it can be inferred that strong interface interactions between Bi2MoO6 and BPB lead to electron transfer from Bi2MoO6 to BPB in the composites [57,58].

Fig. 6 and Table S1 present the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm and pore size distribution curves for Bi2MoO6, BPB and BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4). The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of BPB and BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) in Fig. 6a exhibit type IV isotherms with H4-type hysteresis loops, indicating the presence of mesoporous structures that facilitate the transfer of pollutants [59]. Conversely, the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of Bi2MoO6 were classified as type III isotherms, suggesting the absence of surface pores on Bi2MoO6. Micropores were found to be advantageous for increasing specific surface area and forming an electric double layer. These findings align with the pore size distribution results presented in Fig. 6b. The BET surface area of BPB was notably high at 1113.39 m2·g−1, suggesting its potential as a carrier and adsorbent in heterojunction applications. Upon the addition of Bi2MoO6, there was a significant decrease in the BET surface area to 90.19 m2·g−1, likely attributed to the deposition of Bi2MoO6 nanoplates on the BPB surface, leading to pore structure obstruction. Conversely, the BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) composite demonstrated a larger specific surface area compared to Bi2MoO6 alone (9.77 m2·g−1) along with a relatively higher pore volume, as shown in Table S1. This enhancement not only significantly improved the catalyst's adsorption performance but also promoted the exposure of a greater number of active sites. Furthermore, the presence of BPB in the system resulted in a high adsorption capacity for CIP, attributed to the robust π-π stacking interaction between CIP molecules and the aromatic domains of BPB.

The activity of BPB, Bi2MoO6 and BPB/Bi2MoO6 composites was first assessed by the photodegradation efficiency of CIP in pure water under visible light. To evaluate the efficiency of the samples in photocatalytic reactions, CIP was chosen as the indicator for organic contaminants in artificial seawater. After reaching adsorption equilibrium, the changes in CIP concentration (C/C0) were observed with different durations of illumination for all photocatalysts. When no photocatalyst was used, there was no significant decrease in CIP under visible light exposure, indicating that CIP remained stable and self-photolysis was not a factor. The photocatalytic degradation of CIP with BPB showed a slight increase from 45.4 % in the dark to 47.5 % under light irradiation. This suggests that BPB primarily acts as an adsorbent and lacks photocatalytic properties due to its stability under light. On the other hand, the use of pure Bi2MoO6 as the catalyst led to a gradual decrease in CIP concentration, resulting in a degradation efficiency of 29.8 % after 90 min of irradiation. However, incorporating BPC significantly enhanced the photocatalytic degradation performance of the BPB/Bi2MoO6 samples. The BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) sample achieved a degradation efficiency of 98.7 % after 90 min of irradiation. Conversely, increasing the BPB content in BPB/Bi2MoO6 composites (BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:2)) led to a notable decrease in photocatalytic activity. This emphasizes the importance of maintaining an optimal BPB content for optimal photocatalytic performance. The excessive presence of residual BPB can result in the formation of aggregates that cover the surface of Bi2MoO6, partially hindering light absorption and reducing the efficiency of CIP photocatalytic degradation. Additionally, it is widely known that carbon-based materials have the capability to capture CIP molecules on the surfaces of the composites, with the adsorption capacity increasing as the carbon levels rise. Therefore, surpassing the carbon content present in BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4), results in an excess of CIP molecules being captured on BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:2) surfaces, leading to a partial inhibition of charge transfer at the interface and subsequently decreasing the efficiency of CIP photodegradation [61]. The research findings on reaction rates suggest that the degradation of CIP using these catalysts follows pseudo-first-order kinetics, as depicted in Fig. 8 b. The experimental results indicate a significant improvement in the efficiency of CIP degradation through the integration of BPB into Bi2MoO6, with BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) exhibiting a substantially higher rate constant of 0.0486 min−1 compared to pure Bi2MoO6 with a rate constant of 0.0039 min−1. This enhancement is estimated to be approximately 12.5 times higher, underscoring the effective role of BPB in enhancing photocatalytic performance. The experiment utilized artificial seawater instead of pure water to assess the degradation of CIP in a marine setting with a photocatalyst. In artificial seawater, the efficiency of CIP removal decreased slightly due to the presence of inorganic salts, which hindered the photocatalyst's degradation activity. The photodegradation efficiency of CIP by Bi2MoO6 and BPB/Bi2MoO6 composites at varying ratios (1:10, 1:8, 1:6, 1:4, 1:2) in artificial seawater under visible light irradiation for 90 min was 19.8 %, 49.4 %, 69.5 %, 82.1 %, 93.6 %, and 75.1 %, respectively. This suggests that as the BPB mass increased, the CIP removal rates of BPB/Bi2MoO6 initially increased and then decreased, mirroring the trends observed in a pure water environment. Moreover, the kinetics of CIP degradation in artificial seawater were analyzed using a pseudo-first-order kinetic formula, revealing that the rate constant for BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) was 0.0306 min−1, which was 12.2 times higher than that of Bi2MoO6 (0.0025 min−1). Overall, these findings indicate a slight decrease in CIP degradation over photocatalytic materials in artificial seawater, with BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) maintaining a removal rate of over 90 %, showcasing its suitability for marine environment.

In order to assess the most effective quantity of BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) photocatalyst for breaking down CIP, different amounts ranging from 0.1 to 1.0 g·L-1 were tested. As displayed in Fig. 9 a, the degradation rate of CIP progressively rises from 73.1 % to 98.3 % over 120 min as the quantity of BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) added increases from 0.1 to 0.5 g·L-1. Conversely, the rate drops to 80.3 % within the same time frame when the photocatalyst concentration rises from 0.5 to 1.0 g·L-1. The reduced efficiency at lower levels of photocatalyst is due to the limited availability of active sites on the BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) surface. Clearly, increasing the quantity of BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) improves the number of active sites for CIP adsorption as well as enhances light absorption for generating reactive species, hence improving photocatalytic effectiveness. However, adding high concentrations of photocatalyst at 0.8–1.0 g·L-1 can block light scattering, hindering light penetration through the solution and resulting in decreased degradation efficiency and rate [62]. The removal efficiency of photocatalyst under different pollutant concentrations was studied. In Fig. 9b, it is observed that the degradation efficiency of CIP decreased gradually from 98.3 % to 88.5 % over 120 min as the contaminant concentration rose from 10 mg·L-1 to 20 mg·L-1. Subsequently, a significant decrease in CIP degradation efficiency was noted as the contaminant concentration increased from 20 mg·L-1 to 40 mg·L-1. This decline was attributed to the higher concentration of contaminants impeding the entry of carriers into photocatalytic reactions and the generation of active substances. Moreover, intermediate products formed during the process could outcompete the original contaminants at higher initial concentrations, occupying surface-active sites and further hindering CIP degradation efficiency [63]. In the actual seawater environment, the change of pH will greatly influence photocatalytic properties. Fig. 9c shows the degradation efficiency of CIP at BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) with different pH values (pH = 6, 7, 8, 9). The pH value has a significant effect on CIP degradation. The CIP degradation efficiency was only 69.3 % when pH = 6 and the solution was acidic. The degradation efficiency reaches 98.3 % when the pH rises to 8 in an alkaline environment. However, the photodegradation efficiency of CIP decreases to 80.5 % when pH increases to 9, clearly indicating that pH 8 is the optimal pH value for the photodegradation of CIP by BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4). Seawater contains numerous inorganic salts, and while BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) demonstrates greater adaptability to the marine environment, the presence of predominant salts in seawater can impact the photodegradation of CIP. To assess the influence of key seawater components (NaCl, MgCl2, Na2SO4, and CaCl2) on the photodegradation efficiency of BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4), different salts were excluded in the construction of simulated seawater solutions. As depicted in Fig. 9d, the inclusion of various inorganic salts does not notably alter the photocatalytic performance, with NaCl exhibiting a significant impact on photodegradation (4.3 %), while calcium chloride exerts a minor effect (1.3 %). Overall, the elevated levels of sodium chloride, magnesium chloride, sodium sulfate, and calcium chloride minimally influence the practical utility of photocatalysts, affirming that the synthesized BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) possesses exceptional resistance to saline environments, making it well-suited for effective pollutant degradation in marine settings.

In the practical usage of photocatalysts for water purification, it is vital for them to not only demonstrate high efficiency but also exceptional stability to support repeated applications. In this regard, the stability and reuse potential of the BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) photocatalyst were assessed over five consecutive reaction cycles (dosage: 0.2 g. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the photocatalytic efficacy of the BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) sample remained mostly unchanged even after undergoing five cycles. Although a slight decline in activity was observed, this could be attributed to the inevitable loss of catalyst material during the recycling process. These findings serve as additional proof of the remarkable reusability and stability displayed by the BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) photocatalyst. The XRD patterns and TEM image of BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) post-photocatalytic reaction are displayed in Fig. 10b and Fig. S5a, respectively, demonstrating the well-preserved crystalline structure and morphology of BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) along with its notable stability. To investigate further, the leaching of Bi and Mo ions in the reaction solution was analyzed using atomic absorption spectrophotometry (Fig. S5b). It was observed that during the photocatalytic process, some Bi and Mo ions leached from the material. After 120 min, the maximum leaching concentrations of Bi and Mo ions in the material were 0.28 mg·L-1 and 0.07 mg·L-1, respectively, providing additional evidence of the good stability of BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4). A study on the photocatalytic degradation of CIP in artificial seawater using a continuous flow reaction system (refer to Fig. 2) was conducted to evaluate the effectiveness of BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) technology. Results in Fig. S6 indicate a rapid decrease in CIP concentration within 60 min, attributed to CIP molecules migrating to the surfaces of Bi2MoO6 and BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4). The dynamic rate of CIP treated by Bi2MoO6 is 5.9 % after a 10-hour continuous flow reaction. This value significantly increases to 38.3 % when using BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4), indicating the importance of the synergy between adsorption and photocatalysis. This finding has implications not only in academic laboratory research but also in industrial applications.

The separation and transfer of photogenerated charge can be assessed using various techniques such as photoluminescence spectra (PL), photocurrent responses, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The PL spectra in Fig. 11 a reveal that the introduction of BPB in the BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) composite significantly reduces the luminescence peak centered around 470 nm, indicating a suppression of carrier recombination. This suggests that the incorporation of BPB plays a crucial role in enhancing the charge separation process within the composite. Additionally, Fig. 11b shows that BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) demonstrates a higher photocurrent density compared to pure Bi2MoO6, signifying improved charge transfer capability. This enhancement is attributed to the strong interaction between Bi2MoO6 and BPB, which promotes efficient photocarrier transfer within the composite. Furthermore, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis in Fig. 11c confirms the improved interfacial charge separation in the binary heterojunction system. The smaller Nyquist circle's arc radius for BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) suggests reduced resistance in carrier transfer, leading to enhanced migration of photogenerated electrons and holes within the respective components. This efficient charge separation mechanism effectively suppresses recombination, allowing a higher number of free charges to participate in the photocatalytic degradation process. Overall, the synergistic effects between Bi2MoO6 and BPB in the composite enhance the overall photocatalytic activity by facilitating efficient charge transfer and reducing carrier recombination. The contribution of active species to the removal ability of BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) was assessed by conducting radicals quenching experiments. Scavengers such as isopropyl alcohol (IPA), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt dihydrate (EDTA-2Na), and p-benzoquinone (BQ) were used to quench •OH, hole (h +) and •O2 – radicals, respectively (Fig. 11d). The findings revealed that the addition of BQ (1 mM) resulted in a slight reduction in photocatalytic efficiency, leading to a decrease in degradation rate of 6.1 % in the presence of CIP solution. This suggests that •O2 – has a minimal impact on the degradation reaction. Conversely, the introduction of EDTA-2Na (1 mM) and IPA (1 mM) led to significant reductions of 31.9 % and 46.1 % in the degradation rate of CIP, emphasizing the important roles of h + and •OH in enhancing the photodegradation of CIP over BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) [15]. Additionally, ESR spectroscopy was utilized to confirm the presence of active species. Fig. 11e showed no DMPO-•OH signals in the dark for both Bi2MoO6 and BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) samples. However, after 10 min of irradiation, a distinct peak with a relative intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 was identified as the characteristic DMPO-•OH peak. The intensity of the DMPO-•OH peak in BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) was higher compared to Bi2MoO6, suggesting a greater generation of •OH radicals during the photocatalytic degradation process over BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4). In Fig. 11f, it was observed that no DMPO-•O2 – signals were detected in the dark and light for both catalysts, indicating the inability of these catalysts to produce •O2 –. Quenching experiments and ESR analysis revealed that •OH and h+ were the primary active species responsible for the degradation of CIP.

From an atomic perspective, in order to gain a deeper understanding of how the electrical conductivity is enhanced by the heterogeneous interface of BPB/Bi2MoO6, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were employed. The crystal structure diagrams of Bi2MoO6 and BPB/Bi2MoO6 can be seen in Fig. 12 a. To further investigate the electrical conductivity of BPB/Bi2MoO6, work function analysis was carried out. The introduction of BPB resulted in a decrease in the work function value from around 6.51 eV to 4.68 eV as shown in Fig. 12b and c, indicating that the formation of a heterogeneous interface between Bi2MoO6 and BPB effectively enhances electron transport and improves the electrochemical properties [64]. The density of states (DOS) for Bi2MoO6 and BPB/Bi2MoO6 is illustrated in Fig. 12d and e. As depicted in Fig. 12d, the valence band top of Bi2MoO6 is mainly influenced by the electron orbital hybridization between Bi and O atoms, while the contribution of Mo and O atoms is more apparent towards the bottom of the conduction band, which aligns with previous computational study [65]. Upon the addition of BPB (Fig. 12e), a noticeable leftward shift in the overall energy distribution of BPB/Bi2MoO6′s density of states is observed, indicating a significant interaction between BPB and Bi2MoO6 [66]. The position of the valence band top was also changed due to the contribution of carbon atoms, which also well verified the experimental results, that was, after carbon introduction the energy levels of the valence band top and conduction band bottom of Bi2MoO6 changed to a certain extent, which provided important theoretical support for the effect of BPB incorporation on Bi2MoO6 at the electronic level [67,68]. Furthermore, to validate the impact of the internal electric field, the charge density difference at the interface between BPB and Bi2MoO6 was calculated (Fig. 12f). The presence of the yellow area signifies electron accumulation, while the green area indicates charge depletion. At the heterostructure interface of BPB/Bi2MoO6, the surface of BPB is predominantly covered with the yellow area, indicating an accumulation of electrons on the surface of BPB.

Based on the preceding discussion and theoretical calculations, a plausible photocatalytic degradation mechanism of BPB/Bi2MoO6 under visible light irradiation is illustrated in Fig. 13 . Initially, in the absence of light, CIP molecules exhibit rapid adsorption onto the BPB/Bi2MoO6 surface owing to the high specific surface area of BPB. The elevated concentration of CIP surrounding the catalyst facilitates the subsequent photocatalytic oxidation process. Upon exposure to visible light, the generation of electrons and holes from Bi2MoO6 occurs. Notably, the electron from Bi2MoO6 can efficiently transfer to BPB at the heterojunction interface, leveraging the excellent electrical conductivity (similar with graphite, Fig. S8) and porous structure of BPB to enhance the separation of electron-hole pairs and augment photocatalytic efficiency. Given that the valence band potential of Bi2MoO6 surpasses that of •OH/OH– (2.38 vs. NHE), the production of •OH with potent oxidation capabilities ensues, leading to the oxidation of CIP molecules. Additionally, the photo-induced h+ of Bi2MoO6 play a pivotal role in directly degrading the CIP molecules. Through the combined oxidizing actions of h+ and •OH, CIP molecules undergo degradation, ultimately transforming into CO2 and H2O. These multifaceted synergistic interactions collectively enhance the photocatalytic performance of BPB/Bi2MoO6.

Determining the generated intermediates from the photodegradation process is a crucial step in understanding the degradation pathway. Fig. S7 and Table S2 display the mass spectra and chemical structure of the intermediates produced during the photodegradation of CIP. LC-MS results indicate that seven identifiable intermediates were produced, labeled A-G with protonated molecular ions at m/z = 362, 344, 334, 306, 291, 263, 245, respectively. Three potential degradation pathways are depicted in Fig. 14 , based on the structural characteristics of CIP. In pathway Ⅰ, piperazine underwent oxidation, losing a formaldehyde group to produce C1 and C2 isomers with m/z = 334. Subsequently, C1 and C2 gave rise to D (m/z = 306) by losing formaldehyde. D then underwent secondary amine nitrogen loss, oxidation, and further formaldehyde loss from the amine side chain, resulting in the formation of E (m/z = 291) and F (m/z = 263). This transformation process aligns with previous studies on CIP degradation [69]. Eventually, F was reduced and defluorinated to form intermediate G (m/z = 245), which further degraded into CO2, H2O, and other small molecules [70]. Pathway Ⅱ involved the opening of the piperazine ring through oxidation, leading to the production of the dialdehyde derivative A (m/z = 362). The defluorination of A gave rise to B (m/z = 344). Continuous oxidation and bond cleavage led to a decrease in molecular weight of intermediate products, ultimately forming G (m/z = 245) and demonstrating the effective decomposition of CIP in the photocatalytic ozonation process. In pathway Ⅲ, CIP was oxidized into A with protonated forms (m/z = 362), which further transformed into C1 or C2 (m/z = 334) through the loss of a carbonyl group. The subsequent degradation pathway was consistent with pathway I. The developmental toxicity and mutagenicity consensus of CIP and its intermediates were evaluated using toxicity assessment software (T. E. S. T. v 5. 1. 1), as depicted in Fig. S9. Fig. S9a illustrates a significant reduction in the developmental toxicity of the degradation intermediates compared to CIP. Additionally, the teratogenicity of most degraded products (A, B, C1, C2, D, E, F, and G) was found to be “mutagenicity negative” and decreased notably when compared to CIP (Fig. S9b). These findings indicate that the degradation of CIP by BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) effectively reduces the overall ecotoxicity of CIP.

In summary, the successful synthesis of BPB/Bi2MoO6 composites was achieved using a straightforward hydrothermal method for removing CIP, with banana peels utilized as the starting materials for creating BPB. The BPB/Bi2MoO6 photocatalysts demonstrated excellent light absorption in the visible spectrum, effective separation of photoinduced carriers, and outstanding performance in degrading CIP antibiotics under simulated seawater conditions. This enhanced photocatalytic activity can be attributed to the optimized interaction between Bi2MoO6 and BPB at the interface, as well as the distribution of Bi2MoO6 on the surface of BPB, facilitating rapid transfer of charge carriers. The scavenger experiments and ESR tests indicated that •OH and h+ played key roles in the photocatalytic degradation process. Furthermore, BPB/Bi2MoO6 (1:4) exhibited strong resistance to degradation under visible light irradiation. LC-MS testing was conducted to analyze the intermediate products formed during degradation, leading to proposed pathways for the breakdown of CIP. Additionally, the reaction mechanism in this photocatalytic process was verified using DFT calculations, further supporting the proposed degradation pathway of CIP. Overall, this study highlights the potential of BPB/Bi2MoO6 composites as effective materials for eliminating organic pollutants in seawater through a combination of efficient adsorption and photocatalytic degradation.
